narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like grades, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 (below the average of 3.21), LSAT score of 29 (well under the typical 36.7), middle family income bracket, full-time student status, and school deciles of 5th in Year 1 and 3rd in Year 3—and predicted a 45.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their strong pass rates. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3rd, which is notably lower than the average 5.53 and signaled weaker performance relative to peers in the dataset. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your undergraduate GPA, which had a strong favorable pull toward predicting bar exam success, performing better than it might in other contexts despite being below average. Your Year 1 decile of 5th also nudged the prediction positively toward passing, aligning closer to the typical 5.71 average and showing steadier early performance. Overall, these top three academic ranking factors outweighed others like your LSAT or family income in shaping the model's close-call decision against you. Compared to typical students in the dataset—who often enter with higher deciles, GPAs around 3.21, and LSATs near 37—your profile showed solid but not standout academics, especially in later years, placing you in a riskier group for bar passage. While full-time status is common (over 92% of students), it didn't sway things much here. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly above average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying next cycle with a narrative highlighting your resilience as an Asian male overcoming these metrics. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by targeting schools with holistic reviews or improving one key area dramatically. You're close—refining these elements could flip the prediction in your favor. Keep pushing; your determination will shine through.
